text: High-intensity interval training (HIT) is one of the most effective means of improving cardiorespiratory and metabolic function and physical performance in athletes.

An optimal HIT stimulus to elicit both maximal cardiovascular and peripheral adaptations requires athletes to spend at least several minutes per session at or above 90% of their maximal oxygen uptake (VO2max).
Time accumulated above 90% of VO2max (T@VO2max) during a session is a key variable for characterizing the effectiveness of HIT programming.

HIT prescription involves the manipulation of up to nine variables: work interval intensity, work interval duration, relief interval intensity, relief interval duration, exercise modality, number of repetitions, number of series, between-series recovery duration, and between-series recovery intensity.
Including sprints and all-out efforts in HIT programmes is an effective practice for improving performance in team and racquet sport players.

Beyond T@VO2max, HIT programming should also consider cardiovascular work, anaerobic glycolytic energy contribution, acute neuromuscular load, and musculoskeletal strain to fully characterize the training stimulus.
